speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hey, Mrs. Huppert. In-- in closing, why was it important for you to give your testimony now when you've never given it before?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, we didn't have the opportunity. Some people did not have interest in this, in our past. I guess that just nobody wanted to even to speak about it. I think it was too painful. To me, we cried every day. But I don't need consultation. I just feel that I want our people to remember my children, my grandchildren-- I have only one. [? Martyr, ?] like, to tell them, to remember the horror. Not to forget that our parents, and all the family, and every one of us, that their suffering didn't go-- didn't-- went to vain-- [INAUDIBLE] didn't go in vain. And for the six million innocent souls, children, and-- and-- and babies who have-- all our 6 million [? martyrs, ?] the world [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] profane the holy name. They ruin the-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] they-- they broke the Ten Commandments. Thy shall not kill. And for-- for all this suffering and more, the whole world be accounted yet for. But what I want to wish now, I hope the world will learn to be less-- to live with each other, to-- for some reason, one thing we have to remember, never to forget. Because of that, it will give us strength to go on. And by remembering what happened to us, thank God we have Israel. Because if we would have had Israel, this would have never happened. And I hope our people won't forget to be strong. Because strong-- when we strong-- when we weak, the whole world is like vultures. The weak has no place in this world. They're like-- the world is like vultures waiting just for the prey. But by remembering that, we will avoid this tragedy, what happened to our people, that never in the world ever before, no one ever went through what we went through, experienced this kind of horror. And I hope they will never again, which I hope with this will-- that's why I want to teach my people and my generation to come, not to forget. And to continue Judaism, the tradition, to remember about their grandparents, what happened, the culture. That we have-- that we've been people, not just numbers. And there were the-- the whole-- I think that [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the intelligence and everything we had was gone. But by renewing now with our generation, that it will continue forever, here to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else in closing that you'd like to end with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have-- someone requested, because being kin knowing that I have a good voice, she wrote-- I forgot her name. Very bright young lady. She-- she wrote a song in German, in German language. And I hope to translate this in English. I feel that those words had a lot of meaning, that we should all also remember what been all about, and what we should not to forget to be what we are, and how it was because of this. So may I say it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN]. That means, Jew, where are you going? [GERMAN]. I will just say the lyric, rather than-- I cannot sing anymore. I'm just translating. [GERMAN]. That means, you have no homeland. [GERMAN]. You are in the world as a human being, not recognize. [GERMAN]. They push you a new life here and there.

speaker: interviewee
text: For you-- [GERMAN]. That means, in you-- for you is nowhere empty space. [GERMAN]. We have to fight. [GERMAN]-- The world to show-- [GERMAN]-- That we are here. [GERMAN]. Our people. [GERMAN]. That means, in spite of the wind and weather-- [GERMAN]-- You won't give up. [GERMAN]. Because great is our God. [CRYING] That's the finish. For great is our-- because great is our God to remain a Jew. That's it. That's the song. She asked me sing it. And I thought she deserved to be at least-- if an even unknown writer of this song, I think she deserves at least to be-- talked, just talked about. Because that was a beautiful way of expression, how we've been and what we are, and not to forget how important this to-- how it is to be strong, and not to be afraid, not to dismay. To be full of courage. That's the only-- that's the only way we can survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you'd like to-- to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: For our children, I don't. I learned about the picture later. Would you like to this now or later? Would it be too late for you? When I received the-- the picture from my grandparents, from grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll-- we'll-- we'll-- we'll do the pictures next.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'll do this later. And about my children, I think I will then say something while they're here, if you don't mind. I hope they can make it, because Morris says it's so bad outside-- foggy and rainy. But that day they cried together with us. The heavens cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Mrs. Huppert, thank you very, very much for offering your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for you going. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: We will meet your family. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I appreciate your time and effort and going through the torture with us, and listening all this-- this what you went through. But I think the world should know what happened to our people. And to avoid the tragedy, we have to know, and to learn, to be reminded often, because we forget. Our people forget often, and this is our tragedy.

speaker: interviewee
text: We take everything, accept so quickly, and forget. We love it. But then while it happened, then we remember. But soon, it's-- it's-- it's over, then we forget. And that's what's-- that's wrong. And I'm glad that you have at least-- having people wonderful like you are, to bring it back and, like you say, from California, which I know his mother is a survivor as well. Steinberg? Spielberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Spielberg. And I think you deserve all the credit for making this effort to gather all the thoughts, and all the people, what there is left from us, to make this statement, and to make this valuable tape forever to remember, to remain, to remind the world to change, to be more civilized. I think they have a lot to learn, this world. It's a beautiful world, but the people have to change in order to make a better place to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mrs. Huppert.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Firstly, Mrs. Huppert, could you introduce us to this beautiful family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my wonderful family. God blessed me with them. It's first of all, my husband, my dear husband, which I love and hope to be with a long time, God give us a long life and happy. Something to comfort, to look forward, better taste, better life. At least the generation to come, the one what we have, to see something to progress and to be proud of, which we are right now. This is my husband, Moris Huppert. This is my only daughter, Rita Huppert-- Rita Frumin. And this is my son-in-law, Harvey Frumin. This is my grandson, Zaron Frumin and Jonathon Frumin, the younger one. And they are the balm of-- for my-- of our wounds. Looking at them, it's the only comfort we have. And we hope that our generation, that our tradition will go on. That the-- the parents, the souls of my parents and family what they perished didn't go in vain. And I hope they will remember that. To be strong, not dismay. And if we remember, that's the only way we can avoid the tragedy we went through. And we hope that their life will be more secure and less painful than we went through in our childhood, not even having our teenage life. I hope they value that. I hope they appreciate the freedom they have to live. And as long as we have Israel, the strength we get, this through the strength we should give us to go on in life. Because never again it can happen like this. And as long as we remember, and they will remember to tell their children and grandchildren what happened to our family, to our parents, our blessed memory, that will never happen again. To be strong. Because when you're weak, the world is like vultures for the-- going for the prey. As long as we continue to be strong, this will never happen again. And maybe until the human race will learn to live with each other in peace. And at meantime, you got to be strong, and to continue our tradition. Not to forget that you are a Jew like my [NON-ENGLISH] to be a Jew, not to forget there's a God. Remember that. That's the-- that's the last word I got from my parents, and I'm repeating to you all to continue that. This is what our-- our destiny is, and the reason that we've been saved and our life, what we are living now, to continue the tradition.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was beautiful. Mr. Huppert?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's not much left for me to say. She said it all. [LAUGHTER] The only thing I can add is, I have 50 wonderful years with my wife. And I hope we will be able to live another like my great grandfather. They say he lived 125, and the cousin in Australia. The other one in New York says only 105. So whatever, if there is 105, that's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] our children-- Jonathon, Rita, and Harvey, and Zron, and Jonathon. We're very proud of Rita, Harvey, Zaron, and Jonathon, also. And especially Rita and Harvey did a beautiful job. They give us two wonderful grandsons, [INTERPOSING VOICES] which they're the greatest. I've no words for them. They accomplished more than I expected, much more than I expected. May God bless them. And she-- like my wife said, to be strong. And the Bible says, [NON-ENGLISH]. From generation to generation, to do good deeds in the world, and to see there will be peace. It's a wonderful country. The United States gave us a good break. This is the best country in the world. Everybody wants to come to this country, to the United States. So the only thing I can say to them, shalom, and to live, and to be strong, and not to worry about anything. And I'm sure they'll-- they'll do-- they'll go the footsteps of our forefathers.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's beautiful, thank you. Rita, is there anything you'd like to say to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a lot that I want to say, but maybe I should wait. The boys wanted to say something first.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: May I then afterwards?

speaker: interviewer
text: Certainly. Jonathon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I'm going to start. This is a--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Zaron.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an excerpt from an essay that both of us had written a while back, but we think that it-- we know that it expresses what we want to say very clearly, so. We could not believe that one people could hate another so much, that those people would attempt to destroy an entire culture. The fact that neither of us would be here today had our grandparents not survived made us both realize that the Holocaust was much more than just a horrible event that happened to our people. Our very existence is directly connected to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to you both, we just want to say that we've always felt a strong sense of admiration towards both of you, and for all you've been able to accomplish despite everything you've been through. And we are always-- we are very deeply moved that you've always expressed a strong commitment to Judaism and to God, and have been able to instill-- instill that into us, as well. And so for that, we are tremendously thankful, and we love you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're remarkable people. You put your lives on the line for your family and for people that you saved during the war. And [? it's ?] selflessly you've done those things, for all of us and for them. And to us, you're unsung heroes, but absolutely heroes in our eyes. And we just-- it's an honor for us to be able to carry on everything that you believe for the future, and for our families, and for our generations that will follow us. So that's what I wanted to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's-- that's pretty good. Harvey?

speaker: interviewee
text: To sort of repeat some of the same ideas, I had mentally made some notes. I think that both Moris and Rose have been strong influences for our belief and strengths in Judaism, and also for love and caring for each other, and for one another, and helping others. And also for education. They've been a strong supporter of-- of education, which our boys have-- still pursuing at this time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And a strong, important influence in all of our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very nice, and Rita? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you could speak up a little more, Rita?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I guess there really aren't enough words to adequately express the kinds of emotions and gratitude that we feel in our heart. You are both exemplary individuals, outstanding. I think you're like-- they speak of the Lamed-Vavniks, the 36 righteous, in whose merit the world continues to exist. And in our eyes, you are that. And not only for what you've gone through and what you've endured all these years, but you continue with your moral courage and to shine the light of truth, and to be your guidance and the example that you give not only for all of us, but to all who come to know you. And for too long, your valor, and your honor, and your heroism has gone unrecognized. And you deserve [NON-ENGLISH], every honor. And we are thankful to Hashem, to God, for giving us this-- the precious blessing and gift of your lives and your example. And I hope we can live up to your expectations, and to Hashem's expectations of us. And you continue to inspire. And to us, you're like the two-- two diamonds, forged under the most extreme, unbelievable, enormous, pressure.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry, daddy. I didn't mean to do that to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] And-- and during-- withstanding the-- all-- all that life has thrown in-- thrown in your way, and yet you emerge [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] still brilliant, shining the light of hope, and truth, and honor. And I don't know how you were able to do what you've been able to do all these years, despite the tremendous odds that you've had to face and overcome. Thank you so much, thank you, Hashem. May you be blessed. May our children be blessed always. And we have a tremendous commitment and a sacred trust, a tremendous legacy in keeping the Shoah, and the lessons of the Shoah, the Holocaust, alive. And the cornerstone of our faith is the Shema because our people have always said three times the Shema, and the dying lips of those that perished also uttered the Shema. And the Shema contains two letters enlarged, ayin and dalet. And together they form the words, witness, and we are a witness. And when we reverse the letters, it means [NON-ENGLISH], you shall know. Know. And I think there is a second Shema when we speak of the Holocaust. And we were spared, if I may read something that I've included in past Yom Ha'Shoah commemorations. And this was only a portion, but I hope it will be a fitting expression. We were not spared in order to live in slavery and oppression, to await some new Auschwitz, Treblinka, Belzec. There is life that is worse than death, and death greater than life. We were spared for Torah in order to bear witness and to ensure life, and freedom, and honor for ourselves, for our children, and their children. Shema Israel, hear, oh, Israel. Learn, oh, Israel. [NON-ENGLISH]. And you shall teach them diligently to thy children. [NON-ENGLISH]. Remember to remain a Jew. Remember, for we are too few. Destruction and degradation, the cost of our near annihilation, were too high a price to establish Israel as a nation. Those cherished souls who perished, lost in Holocaust, it must not be all for naught. It must not be a hollow cost. And I pray that God will give us togetherness many years and strength. And that your children, you will help transmit the sacred trust of Torah, and the memories, and the teachings of our people, and of your parents and grandparents. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you all very much. Thank you, Mrs. Huppert. Thank you Mr. Huppert. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: What, another kiss? All kisses are sweet. All sweet kisses. All sweet kisses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Huppert, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather, my daddy's father, which I never knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what his name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Name was Pavel Itzik. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what country he was born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I assume it was Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- how did you come to have this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to the United States, and a distant relative in Toledo, he told he has a picture my grandfather sent to him. And he was led to give it to me, which I was proud to-- to-- to have. Because I never knew my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the opposite side of this photograph. Why was this important to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think when I saw the handwriting on the other side, it was-- I was very surprised and proud to learn that my grandfather, that in that time, which to people even born Poles, they did not speak grammar or to speak-- or to write that beautiful handwriting with the Gothic lettering. And it's-- it's amazing that in that time and this stature, that being Orthodox, that he expressed so beautifully in this correct language. And I'm very proud and touched that I'm now to-- in possession of this picture, which I really appreciate and cherish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Huppert, who is this a photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mom and daddy which I-- they were engaged. And it happened that I saved this during the war. That's the only possession that's of the-- my parents and the indication I had. And I saved this picture. I made a copy of the original, because the other one already went to pieces. And I'm glad that at least I have some memories to bring back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to save it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I put in my purse between two-- in between-- I opened the-- it was a made-- purse I made in camp. They gave us the rags to clean the machines, and I put in between the-- like, the linings, and that's the way I-- I did not give up. They ask to give everything up. All of the [INAUDIBLE] pictures [INAUDIBLE] and other possessions, which I remained silent, took this chance, and I'm glad I did. Now I'm proud. At least I have something to-- to look backwards at my parents, that I have parents. Because I did never have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this, Mrs. Huppert?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother Joseph, who lives now resides in Paris. And in center, I am, Rose. And on the side is my sister, Frania. My brother went that time. He never went to-- he was always going to private school, the highest school in the country, which they are graded on the highest quality on the education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea when and where this photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Sosnowiec, I think the shelter where we lived as a child. I vaguely remember because we'd been very young that time. All I remember that you should look in the camera, we see a bird will go out. So that's how old we were. That is very young. My brother was, I think 9 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you come to have this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo I got because of my brother who survived in Russia, in Siberia, and he happened to have a few pictures from home with him. And after the war, he made a copy, and I-- that's the way I got this. Otherwise, I'd never have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this a photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mom, blessed memory, my uncle on the left-hand side, the brother of my mom, and center's my aunt, the wife of my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one is your mother? On the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left-hand side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where and when this photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was just before the war in 1939. And they came from visiting someone, that was [NON-ENGLISH] on the bridge as I recall. I can recognize the bridge. And they came from visiting someone. And I met them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this a photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [? I am ?] myself, Rose Huppert. That was during the beginning of the war in-- beginning of 1940, and so my brother asked for pictures to send-- to send from the family. So they made the picture, and it was sent to my brother to Siberia. And that's the way I-- and after the war, he made the copy, which that's the way-- that's the only way I possess this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was taken in--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside Sosnowiec. In shelter-- whatever, I don't recall. Sosnowiec. That was downtown, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this a photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Frania, the youngest sister. And she was just beautiful. We all loved her. And this is the same time when they made mine, she made hers, and we send this to my brother, which he requested. In the same way, I got the copy from him. Otherwise, I would never have anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So again, this was 1940 in Sosnowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beginning '40, yes. It's my recollection. [? I can't ?] exactly pinpoint.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this a photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, only brother, oldest brother, who reside now in Paris. And he's the one who survived in Siberia. And this was taken, the picture, just before the war in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph Zarasinska, Chaim Josef in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this a photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a group of students which I attend with, and these are my teachers. They taught us all kind of subjects. And a friend of mine visiting Israel, she was-- surprised me with the picture, and she gave it to me, and it was very nice of her. And she brought old memories, good-- beautiful memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe where you are in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am in the back [POINTS] of this right here, the blue and white dress. Royal blue dress. It doesn't show the color. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, but it's-- so you're fourth from the right on the top row?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and when was this taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] .

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are we seeing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was from the camp, personal kleider, from the lager. They say lager, Markstadt. And that is the date when I arrived to Markstadt, and also the date of Peterswaldau, the ladies-- the ladies' camp when I arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what was this document to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was just something prove that I was there. And at the time when I arrived, and what camps I was in. And what they tried to give us. They took away our clothes. They gave me some substitutes, whatever they wanted to do, to give. And I thought, this is something valuable to know the world, how to-- that to prove that it exist, that we've been in camp, and that they had the-- the names of the camps I've been. And I thought that would be verifying, original.
